title: How to Turn Dreams into Bold Goals and Live with Purpose as a Woman Leader
guestBio: Wendee Close is an entrepreneur, mother of two, and founder of Goals2Life.com. As a WBENC-certified business owner, she is passionate about empowering others to set bold goals and transform their dreams into actionable steps while supporting women-owned businesses across the country.
excerpt: Discover how entrepreneur and mother Wendee Close transforms dreams into actionable goals while balancing leadership, family, and purpose. Get inspired with practical advice on living life to the fullest and supporting women in business.
metaDescription: Learn how to set bold goals and live with purpose from entrepreneur Wendee Close on The Manage Her podcast. Practical advice for women leaders.
keyTakeaways: Bold goal-setting requires transforming dreams into specific, actionable steps that align with your values and purpose.
Successful women leaders balance family and business by creating systems and being intentional about their time and energy.
Supporting other women-owned businesses creates a powerful network that benefits everyone in the community.
Living life on purpose means making decisions that align with your core values, even when it's challenging.
WBENC certification opens doors for women entrepreneurs and creates opportunities for meaningful business connections.
Motherhood and entrepreneurship can complement each other when you embrace both roles authentically.
Setting boundaries around your goals helps you stay focused on what truly matters most.

pullQuotes.text: When you transform your dreams into actionable steps, you're not just setting goals—you're designing the life you want to live.

content:
What does it really mean to live life on purpose? It's a question that many successful women grapple with as they navigate the complex terrain of leadership, motherhood, and entrepreneurship. In this powerful conversation, Aimee sits down with Wendee Close, a remarkable entrepreneur who has cracked the code on turning dreams into reality while staying true to her values and supporting other women along the way.

As the founder of Goals2Life.com and a proud WBENC-certified business owner, Wendee has built her entire mission around helping others transform their biggest dreams into actionable, achievable goals. But her journey wasn't always straightforward. Like many of us, she had to learn how to balance the demands of growing a business while being present as a mother of two, all while maintaining her commitment to living authentically.

The Art of Bold Goal Setting

There's a significant difference between wishful thinking and bold goal setting, and Wendee has mastered this distinction. She believes that dreams without action plans are simply fantasies, but when you create a bridge between your vision and concrete steps, magic happens. This isn't about setting small, safe goals that you know you can achieve. It's about daring to envision the life you truly want and then reverse-engineering the path to get there.

What makes Wendee's approach so powerful is her emphasis on alignment. Your goals shouldn't just be ambitious—they should be deeply connected to who you are and what matters most to you. When your goals align with your values and purpose, you'll find the motivation to push through obstacles and stay committed even when the path gets challenging.

Redefining Success as a Mother and Entrepreneur

One of the most refreshing aspects of Wendee's story is how she's redefined what success looks like as both a mother and a business owner. Too often, women feel like they have to choose between being fully present for their families or fully committed to their professional goals. Wendee proves that this is a false choice.

The key is intentionality. When you're clear about your priorities and make decisions from that place of clarity, you can show up powerfully in all areas of your life. This doesn't mean perfect balance—that's another myth we need to abandon. Instead, it means being present and engaged in whatever role you're playing in the moment, whether that's leading a business meeting or helping with homework.

Wendee's experience as a mother has actually enhanced her effectiveness as a business leader. The skills you develop as a parent—patience, multitasking, problem-solving under pressure, and the ability to see the big picture while managing daily details—are invaluable in the business world. Rather than viewing motherhood as something that limits your professional potential, you can embrace it as an additional source of strength and wisdom.

The Power of Supporting Other Women

As a WBENC-certified business owner, Wendee understands the importance of creating opportunities for women-owned businesses to thrive. This certification isn't just about meeting requirements—it's about being part of a movement that recognizes the unique value that women bring to the business world and actively works to level the playing field.

Supporting other women in business isn't just the right thing to do; it's also smart business. When women succeed, they tend to lift others up with them, creating a ripple effect that benefits entire communities. Wendee has experienced this firsthand through the connections and opportunities that have come through her WBENC involvement.

This collaborative approach to business stands in stark contrast to the old model of competition and scarcity. When you operate from an abundance mindset and genuinely want to see other women succeed, you create space for everyone to win. Your success doesn't diminish someone else's opportunities—it expands them.

Living Life on Purpose

Perhaps the most compelling aspect of Wendee's message is her commitment to living life on purpose. This means making decisions based on your values rather than external expectations or pressure. It means being willing to take calculated risks in pursuit of what matters most to you. And it means staying connected to your why even when the how gets complicated.

Living on purpose also requires regular check-ins with yourself. Are your current goals still aligned with who you're becoming? Are your daily actions moving you closer to or further from the life you want to create? This isn't about perfectionism—it's about conscious choice-making and course-correcting when necessary.

Wendee's work through Goals2Life.com embodies this principle perfectly. She's not just helping people set any goals—she's helping them identify and pursue the goals that will create genuine fulfillment and impact. This approach transforms goal-setting from a mechanical exercise into a deeply personal journey of growth and discovery.

The conversation with Wendee serves as a powerful reminder that you don't have to choose between different aspects of your identity. You can be a devoted mother, a successful entrepreneur, and a purposeful leader all at the same time. The key is bringing intentionality to each role and allowing them to complement and strengthen each other rather than compete for your attention and energy.

quiz.title: What's Your Goal-Setting Style as a Woman Leader?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach turning dreams into reality and what that reveals about your leadership journey. Based on insights from our conversation with entrepreneur Wendee Close about bold goal-setting and purposeful living.

questions.question: When you think about a big dream you have, what's your first instinct?

options.text: I start mapping out all the steps I'll need to take to get there
I ask myself if this dream aligns with who I want to be and the impact I want to make
I think about different ways I could approach it and what might work with my current situation
I consider who I could collaborate with or how this could benefit other women too

questions.question: You're juggling a demanding work project while your kids need extra attention at home. How do you handle it?

options.text: I create a detailed schedule that blocks time for both and stick to it religiously
I remind myself of my 'why' and make choices that honor both my family values and professional commitments
I get creative with solutions - maybe working early mornings or finding ways to involve the kids
I reach out to my network for support and offer to help others when I can in return

questions.question: What motivates you most when setting goals?

options.text: Having a clear, measurable outcome that I can track and celebrate
Knowing that achieving this goal will help me live more authentically
The excitement of trying something new and seeing what's possible
The opportunity to create something that will benefit other women in my community

questions.question: When you face a setback in pursuing a goal, you typically:

options.text: Analyze what went wrong and adjust my strategy accordingly
Reflect on whether this goal still feels right for who I'm becoming
Look for alternative paths and stay open to unexpected opportunities
Connect with other women who've faced similar challenges for advice and support
Achieving the specific outcomes I set out to accomplish
Living in alignment with my values and making decisions I'm proud of
Having the flexibility to adapt and grow in unexpected ways
Creating positive change that ripples out to benefit other women

questions.question: When sharing your goals with others, you're most likely to:

options.text: Present a detailed plan with timelines and milestones
Talk about the deeper purpose and meaning behind what you're doing
Share your excitement while acknowledging you're still figuring out the details
Invite others to be part of the journey or find ways to support their goals too

results.title: The Strategic Architect
results.description: You're a master planner who loves creating detailed roadmaps to success. You thrive on breaking down big dreams into manageable steps and feel most confident when you have a clear strategy. Your methodical approach helps you balance multiple priorities, whether that's family, career, or personal goals. This episode will inspire you with Wendee's framework for turning ambitious visions into actionable plans that align with your deepest values.

results.title: The Heart-Centered Visionary
results.description: You lead with purpose and passion, making decisions based on what feels authentic to your values. You're naturally drawn to goals that create meaningful impact for others, especially other women. Your strength lies in seeing the bigger picture and inspiring others to join your mission. Wendee's story of building a purpose-driven business while staying true to her values as a mother will deeply resonate with your approach to intentional leadership.

results.title: The Adaptive Innovator
results.description: You excel at pivoting and finding creative solutions when life throws curveballs your way. You understand that success looks different for everyone and you're comfortable carving your own unique path. Your flexibility is your superpower, especially when balancing competing demands. This episode will validate your approach and give you fresh strategies for maintaining focus on what truly matters most while embracing the beautiful chaos of women's leadership.

results.title: The Community Builder
results.description: You believe that rising together is better than rising alone, and you naturally seek to lift other women as you climb. Your goals often involve creating connections, fostering collaboration, and building supportive networks. You understand the power of authentic relationships in achieving success. Wendee's insights about supporting women-owned businesses and creating meaningful connections will fuel your passion for community-driven leadership.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey everyone, I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, Unveiling the Invisible Work and Sparking a Leadership Revolution, hitting Amazon May 1st, 2025. I'm the CEO of Tomahawk Information Solutions, an awesome IT crew pulling in eight figures, and a serial entrepreneur since I was 22. Think real estate flips, prenatal protein powder, and a film production company. I'm a mom of six, and this is The Manager podcast where we're digging into the unseen hustle and sparking some serious leadership vibes.

I'm pumped to chat with Wendee Close, an IT software rockstar, and the brains behind Goals to Life. Wendee is crushing it in a male-heavy field, turning tech into tools and transforming lives, all the while balancing her own crew at home. Hey, Wendee.

**Wendee:** Hey girl, got through it. Yes. That's a mouthful today.

**Aimee:** So you got a chance to read chapter one of The Manager and in that I touch on the invisible work - the labor that we carry for our families and for our companies. How are you affected by that? What does that look like for you?

**Wendee:** Well, it's a different season for me. I'll be quite honest. We just became - I call myself a bird launcher, not an empty nester. We're always half glass full, not empty. So it is a different season right now, but raising both - I have a boy and a girl, two kids, and one's 18 and the other one is 24. And through it all with them both being in sports and juggling being an entrepreneur basically my whole life since they've been young, managing whether I'm working from home, working at the office, trying to be at all the things and take care of a household and make sure that my marriage is completely on point because that's number one. Actually God number one, then my husband, then my kids, then the business.

So what I would say is just really looking at making sure you're touching on all things. Not just so focused on running the business. It's really honestly making sure, okay, what do I have to do to nourish these other areas of my life and really trying to bring awareness to it. I do believe as women, we try to be all for all people and all things. And where I learned is that if I don't take care of myself, I can't take care of anyone else. So in all of that, as we are doing all the do.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Because we do a lot of stuff. I feel like as women, we are kind of the primary manager and organizer of our household. We're definitely the CEO of a house, and there's so many little things that we do and there's so many things that take up mind share. For me right now, I'm thinking, okay, do I need to clean out the kids' closets? Do I need to clean out the toy bin again? It's kind of crazy all of the projects. Did my kids read the book that they need? Did they read the chapters for their English class next week? All of the little things you're just staying on top of so many things.

**Wendee:** Yes. Your brain is full. And I'm detailed - if anyone knows, and I'm actually talking tomorrow about this, but the whole thing about being detailed through and through. I sometimes say they say devil's in the details. I say God is in the details. Like clearly he created the world and all the details of everything we see. But when I say that - is it a blessing or a curse? 

So often being detailed, we see the stuff but then that is the curse - seeing all the details everywhere you go and all the lists aren't going. But it's also a blessing for me because I'm detailed. I'm able to create systems and processes to help myself get through the chaos of all the things that we have to do as a woman and all the hats we wear not only as running a house but running a business and there's just so much.

But what I want to say which touches back on when I did talk about self-care - we also are the manager of ourselves. It starts with us. We have to put our own oxygen mask on first and be a good manager of you. I've actually created a persona of myself where I am my own mother. And I sometimes will check in with my own mother self and be like, would I approve of the way I'm treating myself today?

**Aimee:** Right. And I mean, that's a lot right there. So often you hear women or even hear myself at times, I don't have time to do this for myself because you put everyone else first. And when we're trying to do all the things - and again I think women and mothers just have a different role. There's a lot more moving parts I feel.

**Wendee:** Yeah. And you have to find a way to manage it. When you're so busy, it's so easy to get caught up in - you almost can get buried in it. And I do see a lot of new moms trying to balance - and there's no such thing as balance. Harmony. It's called harmony and systems. You're such a systems person and that's through and through a system. I do think that's how you get through having a newborn and getting through that first year of being a mom - getting your systems down, getting your sleep schedule down, and finding grace too.

So I will tell you the one reason why we created Goals to Life is that we want a system. I don't care if someone's 10 years old or 90, but it's so true even just with the new season of life we're in and me as a woman and me in a marriage and just also me in this business. It's all new seasons. What I'll say is we want Goals to Life to be a life goal plan that is for every season of the year and every season of life.

**Aimee:** I love that. And so while you're in that season of life that is a newborn, we need to understand what are my priorities right now? And how do I make - how do I create the life I want right now in this season?

**Wendee:** Yes. And it's so different because you kind of want to be present. That first year is so important and it's so fast, but when you're in it, it feels like you're going to be stuck there forever. There's aspects of it that are just very tedious and you're just exhausted. But if you could go into a system like Goals to Life and be like, "These are my goals for this year with having this newborn. I want to make sure I get good pictures. I want to spend this amount of quality time with this baby." And if you were able to put your goals into it and then come up with a strategy - it's all about strategies and actions. But you got to write it down.

**Aimee:** Yes, absolutely. And I do think people need to know as they're changing and evolving, so are their intentions, their priorities, their goals. And that's okay. What was important to us in our 20s is not important to us in our 40s or 50s. Even from 40s to 50s, everything changes. Not everything but the foundation of who we are and our integrity and our values and principles. But the priorities of what are we trying to achieve in this season? Or what are we trying to create? What experience?

And especially for women I think because we kind of catch our stride a little later because in our 30s and up to our mid-40s we're still raising small children a lot of the times - I mean I'm still raising small children. We do kind of come into - I feel like there's this little power band right between 40 and 55 where we kind of want to turn it up because we have the bandwidth to do that at that point. Our kids have gotten a little bit bigger. We have a little more free time and there is this new season there where you have this opportunity to go out and build something again.

**Wendee:** Yeah. It feels good. It's empowering I think to feel like there's something that you could do for yourself and make an impact. And I think especially when they start driving. I mean that just releases all the time for people.

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh. Because I am literally a kid taxi. I spend so much time - I'm so glad that I can run my company from my phone because I never sit at my desk ever. I'm taking calls left and right in the car. Always in my car. You see me? I'm zooming. I'm in my car. Sorry. I'm always in my car.

**Wendee:** Do what you got to do. Yeah. I live in it. Yeah. Just safely. Safely. Always. I'm a good driver. I've never gotten - I'm good. No tickets. No accidents. I zoom.

**Aimee:** She's not zooming while she's driving. Maybe she is. I'm not looking into the camera, but I'm there. I'm driving. I'm checking in. I'm really - that's me. I'm talking and I'll be driving.

**Wendee:** Yes. That's so funny. No, but you got to do what you have to do. Seriously. And people understanding that it is a little bit more versatile to just run a business how you need to make the things happen. You got to do what you got to do.

**Aimee:** Well, we're living in a time where technology is kind of optimal for women and the way our lives are because we're really good multitaskers. But now we can really take work with us no matter where we go. I mean, I can be sitting at the baseball field working from my phone. I can be driving kids around. I can have a minute in between this or that. And it's so nice. But you have to plan - the one thing you'll notice is if you're having to do work in the car, you need to know intentionally, "Okay, who do I need to call on this drive?" And have the phone numbers accessible. Have your intentions laid out so you're prepared to be productive.

**Wendee:** Going in with this is what I need to accomplish in this drive and I need to have what I need to have easily accessible so you can be productive on the go. Yeah. Absolutely. Making my to-do lists every morning. That's part of my routine. I feel like that's been part of my success since I was 22. I got really serious about doing my to-do list and checking things off. It feels good. 

And then I read this book called The War of Art. And in that book, they talk about how you take what you want to procrastinate on the most and move it to the top of your list. And that honestly that one little habit - I feel like has really allowed me to succeed in life because once you do the hardest thing, everything else is easy. Everything else is easy. It's such a great thing to make sure.

**Aimee:** Especially if you're making a list, you can pick and I know a lot of these places that have notepads, it will say list of top three things and then your rest of your list. So you would name the one and circle that. And then if you could just make sure the things that matter most, you move that needle forward. I love that, Aimee. I think that's great advice. For me it's been - I don't know if it works for everybody, but for me it sure has been my one little secret.

What's your one little secret to success? For real?

**Wendee:** That's such a good question. My one secret to success is making sure that we're not overloading ourselves with information where we just keep learning and learning without implementing and executing. So easy to do - it becomes very overwhelming. So take one thing at a time. I think the biggest thing is taking what you learn and having a way to put it into a system or a process that could say, "Okay, I just learned this. This matters a lot to me. It's going to move the needle forward." Whether it be in your finances, in your relationship, in your physical health, let me just start making baby steps each day to implement it.

But I'm a big get-it-done girl. My nickname is GSD. So it's about how do we - but the problem is not doing too much. Another tip is knowing when to say no and doing the right things at the right time for who it matters most for. Not doing all for all.

**Aimee:** Yes. And having those boundaries and learning when to say no. Oh, that's such a big one for women too. Learning to say no is so important because you really have to protect your time. Time is everything.

**Wendee:** Time is everything. Everyone has the same 24 hours in a day. My mom - that's such my momism right there. But it's true. So you really do have to guard your time because time is the currency, right? Time is the real currency in everything. And the more as we get older, we value it so much more and we realize how critical.

And that is why one of the biggest things that we've done in Goals to Life is managing time management and focus and distractions. This world is full of the most - and first off, kids are distracting. But when my daughter's home from college, it's so quiet and then all of a sudden you're like, "Mom, dad, this that" - everything shifts and you're like squirrel. And then when you have little ones, forget it. It's constant distractions, let alone the person that doesn't have kids, the world is pulling in a million directions.

**Aimee:** Totally. And then social media and it's so easy to get sucked in and lose your time, right?

**Wendee:** So at Goals to Life, what we've been able to do is take people's plans and intentions and align it with their calendar. We have a full calendar integration tool that goes on the go with them so they know, okay, my intention is between this time to work on my physical activity or to work on my self-care or to work on my relationships. So whatever that is, when they click on it on the time block, it will show them exactly what they want to do. So they're not like, "Okay, first off, what was I supposed to do?"

So if they are on the go, it's like, okay, when it's at baseball time, I'm going to do a quick text check-in with my mom, or I'm going to listen to a podcast that's going to fuel me or I'm going to, whatever it might be. And they're keeping on track with the things that they said they were going to do and make sure it's on their calendar so they actually see progress in each area of their life.

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh. Yeah. Because I have personal goals to check in with my parents once a week and I fall down on those goals all the time. There's always - if something's falling off my to-do list, it's usually that because I don't have time to hop on a call and just chitchat for a half an hour. But I do have a friend who she's a therapist and she has a scheduled call with her mom because her mom lives in South Africa. And she actually comes up with a prompt. So they're not just talking about this and that, but actually comes up with a prompt for the call, gives them a topic to talk about. So they're not just chitchatting - they can actually kind of dive into something a little deeper.

**Wendee:** I love that. I know, right? So you're a little more intentional with your time because once again, it's our time. Just - I don't want to - she doesn't want to hear what her mom's neighbor is doing next door. You can get into these conversations where you're - so often when I'm talking to people, I said, I want to know about what's going on with you and how you're doing. Not necessarily - so I leave the phone feeling connected with you, not what's going on with your five friends or what happened to so and so's child. I care more about you and you talking to me about how you're feeling and what you're doing. Coming into our time with such clear intention of what we want to get out of what we're doing with our time.

**Aimee:** Right. And I thought that - man, I'm like that is a great idea because you do get on the phone and it's like you don't have a - you know when you're in a meeting you have an agenda you're going to go through, you know what you're going over in your meeting, you've got it all laid out. Well it's going into any conversation saying first off I want to have meaningful conversations - that's a huge goal of mine, no surface, I want depth, I want meaningful conversations. That's huge about who I am in my life and relationships, but then on top of it going in with intention. Now, I've never thought about that necessarily on a personal level. I go into meetings always with intention and preparation, but not on the personal level.

**Wendee:** That's a great tip. So I'm going to add that to Goals to Life in our pathways. So if anyone is on the platform and listening, just know that I'll be adding in there some thoughts and maybe I'll add some prompts in there for people to use if they get stuck. Because when we build our goal plan so often people go, "Okay, great. I want to check in with my kids or I want to check in with my parents or grandparents or whomever." But then if you give them ideas exactly on this is why you're doing it. This is where you can go and this is how you do it. It's to reinforce the bond that we have with the people that we're having relationships with. If we're going to spend our time doing this thing being on a call, how can we enforce our bond? How can we make this time that we can actually deepen our relationship?

**Aimee:** Yeah. So relationships are everything. They really are. And I'm really realizing the more women I talk to, the more we're kind of having the same conversation about as the world gets more virtual, we have to be more intentional about creating real relationships in our life.

**Wendee:** I know, right? I'm grateful for you. Grateful for the women in my life that realize the importance of having real life relationships - we are friends IRL. I see my kids and this digital world moving towards online friends, not the in-person. Everyone's preferring text. I love calling people. I want to hear their voices. I want to hear their inflections. But if we're going to do that it is going to take more time and we do want to make sure to go in with intention. What are the few questions I want to ask to really know what's going on so I leave feeling like I connected? You feel like you got deepened in that a little bit and you want to feel like they were deepened by it too. I want to walk away from a phone call with my mom or my dad and feel like they really got something out of it and that I really got something out of it too.

**Aimee:** I love it. Great idea. And this is The Manager. Me writing the book has been like, okay, how can I apply the things that I've learned in business to running my home more efficiently? And but also, how can I take the grace that I run my home with and run my business like that? Because running our homes is so effortless. It's work, but the people around us don't even realize we're doing it most of the time when we're doing it well. A lot of times the kids don't even realize until they move out - "Oh, I had this good." Or, "Wow, a lot was done for me." And they're like, "Oh my gosh, I have to wash dishes and buy toilet paper."

**Wendee:** Well, and then my daughter's like, "Oh, I have to actually cook food because my husband has to eat." Exactly. I mean, I think those are - that's the invisible work, and I think that it's only until you don't have someone doing the invisible work for you. When you move out of your mom and dad's house, things happen. I always go, "How did all this happen? Just poof." I say I'm a fairy. I don't want to make a big deal about it. I'm like the little kitchen fairy. I get up early. I go down. I make sure the kitchen is all picked up and I start my day fresh before the kids come down. I want everything kind of reset and then we can do it again. All right, now we're going to make breakfast. But I can't start my day with five kids in my house on a mess. Just for my personal sanity - the little fairy needs to come first and make sure all these things are done.

**Aimee:** Yeah. For sure. And that's just something I do. I love the title of your book. I think you nailed it with this title. And everything about it feels so good and I'm really excited to let people learn from you and so much that you've experienced in your life.

**Wendee:** Well, and I just feel like the book is going to grow into this community. I really want The Manager to be a community of women where we can kind of be there for each other and be each other's cheerleaders. Women are such good cheerleaders.

**Aimee:** It's a movement too. It's a movement. The Manage Her movement. It really is. I can see us bringing people like you and going on retreats with women and spending quality time together and building real community for women and teaching women how to build community with each other. Because we've kind of lost a lot of the soft skills and especially in the next generation they've really lost their soft skills of how to make real friendships, how to deepen friendships, how to invest your time and be intentional. For the younger generations, it's huge to mentor through that. I know mentorship is a big thing for you.

**Wendee:** Absolutely. It really is. And I think that we have - I feel like through the generations we really have been losing so much of that culture. I was watching a video this morning on Instagram and this man who advocates for women postpartum. And he was talking about how this is the most tired generation of mothers since World War II. And the reason we're so tired is because we're doing it all by ourselves. We don't have the relational - our parents aren't as involved, the grandparents aren't as involved as other generations of grandparents. The aunts and uncles aren't as involved as the last generation.

So we're really kind of doing this by ourselves and it's - doing this alone is not the way that nature intended us. Women are such social animals. We are women in a circle - these sewing circles where we could all sit around and talk. My grandma said if a baby started crying in church back in her day, whoever was lactating would just pick the baby up and pop them on their boob and start nursing that baby. And I'm like, "Wow, we've so gotten too far away from that."

But I have to say again, my neighborhood is this amazing place where this kind of still exists. I was having a trouble with my milk supply and my neighbor Kathleen had a freezer full of milk. So I texted her. I was like, "Kathleen, is there any way I can borrow some of that frozen milk that you're not going to use?" And she's like, "Please come and take it." So I do believe that with intention, with us creating - we're such creators. If we create time and space for women to really have tight social bonds and social circles and we give people the skills, we can do this.

**Wendee:** We can and we have a community too - the Goals to Life community. And what I want to do is if somebody really needs - when we say where do you need the most nourishment in your life right now? Because again, different people may need - they're maybe experiencing stress, anxiety, postpartum depression, whatever it might be. So they need to find someone else that's on their journey that they could have as a peer accountability because we're all about not going through anything alone. We're about how much further we go with others. And with that, it takes community.

And having that when you're trying to achieve or get on the other side of something that you deeply want, need, or desire, you're going to need to have people on that journey with you. And when you do that, that's what Goals to Life is about. 95% of how people will actually achieve what they deeply want in their life is to have someone on that journey encouraging them and motivating and guiding them through.
